text: When designing a REST API, it’s important to follow best practices for clarity and reliability. Start by creating clear and logical endpoints, such as `/users` or `/orders`, that represent resources. Use standard HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE to perform actions. Always return appropriate status codes—for example, 200 for success, 201 for created resources, and 404 when something is not found. Include versioning in your API, such as `/v1/users`, to allow for changes without breaking existing users. Finally, provide thorough documentation that explains each endpoint, expected inputs, and example responses. This helps developers understand and use your API effectively.
